summary: German Late Romantic composer (1864–1949), known above all for his tone poems and operas. Major tone poems include Also Sprach Zarathustra, Ein Heldenleben, Till Eulenspiegel's Merry Pranks, Death and Transfiguration, Don Quixote, and An Alpine Symphony. Operas include Salome, Elektra, Der Rosenkavalier, and Ariadne auf Naxos, several written with librettist Hugo von Hofmannsthal. Late works include Metamorphosen and Four Last Songs.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: German Late Romantic composer (1864–1949). Began career at the Meiningen Court Orchestra as assistant to Hans von Bülow. Co-founded the Salzburg Festival with Hugo von Hofmannsthal and Max Reinhardt. His wife was soprano Pauline de Ahna. Served as president of the Reichsmusikkammer under the Nazis (partly to protect his Jewish daughter-in-law Alice) but was later dismissed after an intercepted letter to Stefan Zweig. Wrote Olympic Hymn for the 1936 Berlin Olympics. Known as a famously relaxed conductor — George Szell said he conducted 'as if he were waiting for the card game that came after.' Called himself 'the last mountain of a large mountain range.'

clues.clue: Giveaway identifiers: German composer of Also Sprach Zarathustra, Salome, Der Rosenkavalier, Elektra, and tone poems like Ein Heldenleben and Till Eulenspiegel's Merry Pranks
examples: name this German composer of Der Rosenkavalier and Salome
name this German composer of tone poems like An Alpine Symphony and Also Sprach Zarathustra

works.name: Also Sprach Zarathustra (Thus Spoke Zarathustra)
works.description: Richard Strauss's 1896 tone poem based on Nietzsche's philosophical work. Famous for its opening 'Sunrise' section (C-G-C brass fanfare used to open Stanley Kubrick's 2001: A Space Odyssey and Elvis Presley concerts). Contains the movements 'Of Science' (fully chromatic fugue), 'The Convalescent,' and 'Song of the Night Wanderer.' Ends with the unresolved juxtaposition of B major (high winds) and C major (low strings). Wikipedia

clues.clue: Opens with an ascending 'C-G-C' (root, fifth, octave) brass fanfare depicting sunrise
examples: A more famous one ends with the 'Song of the Night Wanderer' and begins with the trumpet playing the motif of C-G-C
Its initial 'Sunrise' section features a rising C-G-C brass fanfare

clues.clue: Based on Nietzsche's philosophical work of the same name
examples: Another piece by this composer has a fully chromatic fugue in its 'Of Science' movement and opens with a C-G-C motif depicting sunrise. That tone poem is based on a work of Friedrich Nietzsche's

clues.clue: Its 'Of Science' ('Von der Wissenschaft') movement contains a fully chromatic fugue

clues.clue: Ends with 'Song of the Night Wanderer'; closes with a bitonal clash: B major (high winds/strings) against C major (low strings)
examples: Also sprach Zarathustra ends with the high winds and strings playing a chord in one of these two keys, which is juxtaposed with the low strings plucking the other key's tonic

examples: Herbert von Karajan's recording of a massive, 22-section work by this composer was the first to be pressed on CD

works.name: Ein Heldenleben (A Hero's Life)
works.description: Strauss's 1898 autobiographical tone poem. Composed in E-flat major (the 'heroic' key of Beethoven's Eroica and Emperor Concerto). The six-section work depicts 'The Hero,' 'The Hero's Adversaries' (critics, portrayed by snarling woodwinds/consecutive fifths in winds), 'The Hero's Companion' (solo violin for wife Pauline), 'The Hero's Works of Peace' (quoting earlier Strauss works including Till Eulenspiegel), and 'The Hero's Retirement From This World and Completion.' Tubas in parallel fifths represent the critics. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Six-section autobiographical tone poem; tubas (or woodwinds) playing in consecutive/parallel fifths represent Strauss's critics ('adversaries')
examples: Tubas playing in parallel fifths represent critics of this composer in the 'adversaries' movement of an orchestral work that ends with 'The Hero's Retirement From This World and Completion'
He depicts the Munich critic Doktor Dehring with ominous consecutive fifths in the lower wind and brass

clues.clue: In the 'Works of Peace' section, Strauss quotes from his earlier works (including Till Eulenspiegel and Don Juan)
examples: Strauss copiously quotes his earlier works in the 'Works of Peace' movement of this massive tone poem

clues.clue: Composed in E-flat major — same heroic key as Beethoven's Eroica symphony
examples: Strauss used this 'heroic' key for Ein Heldenleben, as it is the same key Beethoven used for his Eroica symphony and Emperor Piano Concerto

clues.clue: Quotes his own Death and Transfiguration in the tone poem
examples: what late Romantic German composer of Aus Italien quoted his Death and Transfiguration in the tone poem A Hero's Life?

works.name: Also Sprach Zarathustra / An Alpine Symphony – General Tone Poems
works.description: An Alpine Symphony (Eine Alpensinfonie, 1915) is Strauss's 22-section tone poem depicting a day in the Alps — including a Sunrise (similar opening to Zarathustra), The Ascent, On the Summit, and a Thunderstorm and Descent. Despite its name it is a tone poem, not a symphony. Requires wind machine, thunder machine/thundersheet, 12 offstage horns, heckelphone, cowbells, and organ. Herbert von Karajan's recording was the first CD ever pressed. Wikipedia

examples: A work with sections like 'On Flowering Meadows' and 'On the Summit.' ... An Alpine Symphony

works.name: Till Eulenspiegel's Merry Pranks
works.description: Strauss's 1895 tone poem depicting the medieval prankster Till Eulenspiegel. Opens with a 'once upon a time' theme, then presents Till's theme in 6/8 in F major for solo horn. A death sentence by descending seventh in the trombones/tuba is followed by a D clarinet squeal and flute trill as Till is hanged. A solo violin cantilena ends the work, quoting the opening fanfare from Also Sprach Zarathustra. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Opens with a 'once upon a time' theme; Till's main theme is in F major in 6/8 for solo horn
examples: That piece by this composer of Aus Italien depicts the title character with a lilting theme in F major in 6/8 for solo horn, and opens with a 'once upon a time' theme

clues.clue: Till's death: a descending seventh in trombones and tuba pronounces his death sentence; then D clarinet squeals and flute trills as he is hanged

clues.clue: A horn and D clarinet represent the title prankster
examples: in Strauss's tone poem Till Eulenspiegel's Merry Pranks, a horn and a clarinet tuned to this key represent the title prankster

works.name: Death and Transfiguration (Tod und Verklärung)
works.description: Strauss's 1889 tone poem depicting a dying man's experience. Opens with a 'heartbeat' theme — triplets on the dyad E-flat/G marked by frequent gaps, representing a faltering heartbeat. A prominent 'transfiguration motif' (F – B-flat – C – D – high D – C) is introduced first by the brass. The motif is quoted in the Four Last Songs. Premiered along with the Burleske for piano and orchestra. Strauss reportedly said on his deathbed that the piece had accurately depicted death. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Opens with a 'heartbeat' theme — triplets on the dyad E-flat/G with frequent gaps, depicting a faltering heartbeat

clues.clue: The 'transfiguration motif' (F – B-flat – C – D – high D – C) is introduced first by the brass; later quoted in the Four Last Songs

works.name: Don Quixote
works.description: Strauss's 1897 tone poem ('fantastic variations on a theme of knightly character'), Op. 35. Depicts Cervantes's Don Quixote via a theme-and-variations structure. Famous for flutter-tongued horns representing bleating sheep. Also uses oboe and E-flat clarinet flutter-tongue. Leonard Bernstein learned difficult passages from Bruno Walter's sickbed before his breakthrough 1943 Carnegie Hall debut. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Bleating sheep represented by flutter-tongued horns (and E-flat clarinet/oboe)
examples: Bleating sheep are represented using flutter-tongued horns in this tone poem

works.name: Metamorphosen
works.description: Strauss's 1945 work for 23 solo strings, composed after the bombing of the Vienna Opera House. Quotes the funeral march from Beethoven's Eroica Symphony, with 'In Memoriam' written over it in the score. Karl Böhm and Paul Sacher commissioned it so Strauss could afford a spa visit near Zurich. Wikipedia

works.name: Four Last Songs (Vier letzte Lieder)
works.description: Strauss's late song cycle for soprano and orchestra (1948), primarily to texts by Hermann Hesse. Despite its name, it was not the last piece he wrote. Quotes the 'transfiguration motif' from Death and Transfiguration after the soprano sings 'Is this, perhaps, death?' at the end of the final song. Jessye Norman made a heralded recording with Kurt Masur and the Leipzig Gewandhaus Orchestra. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Song cycle primarily to texts by Hermann Hesse; composed shortly before Strauss's death

works.name: Salome
works.description: Strauss's 1905 opera based on Oscar Wilde's play, for which Strauss wrote his own libretto. Depicts the biblical story of Salome, stepdaughter of Herod, who demands the head of John the Baptist (Jochanaan). Famous for the 'Dance of the Seven Veils' striptease and the 'sickening chord' — a polytonal F-sharp major over A dominant 7th — that sounds as Salome kisses the severed head. Opens with a clarinet scale ambiguous between C-sharp major and G major. Wikipedia

clues.clue: The title character performs the 'Dance of the Seven Veils' then demands the head of John the Baptist (Jochanaan) on a silver platter
examples: This composer shocked audiences by having a woman demand that an executioner use a silver platter to bring her a dead man's head before she performs the erotic 'Dance of the Seven Veils'

clues.clue: A polytonal 'sickening chord' (F-sharp major over A dominant 7th) sounds as Salome kisses the severed head; she is then crushed to death on Herod's orders
examples: A polytonal chord made up of an F-sharp major chord over an A dominant 7th chord plays as the title character kisses a freshly severed head, after which they are crushed to death
ends with a 'sickening chord' framed by C-sharp major

clues.clue: Opens with a clarinet scale ambiguous between C-sharp major and G major, followed by Salome's leitmotif
examples: Salome opens with an ambiguous scale moving between C-sharp major and G major played on this instrument

clues.clue: Based on Oscar Wilde's play of the same name; Strauss wrote his own libretto (unlike most of his operas)
examples: This composer collaborated with Hugo von Hoffmansthal on Elektra and Der Rosenkavalier, but wrote his own libretto after an Oscar Wilde play for his opera Salome

clues.clue: Several Jews argue about the nature of God within the opera

works.name: Elektra
works.description: Strauss's 1909 opera (libretto by Hugo von Hofmannsthal), based on Sophocles' tragedy. Begins with four notes 'D, down to A, up to long F, D' representing Agamemnon (Elektra's murdered father). Features the 'Elektra chord' — a dissonant bitonal chord implying both E major and C-sharp major. Ends with Elektra triumphantly dancing herself to death. Patrice Chéreau's final production was for the 2013 Aix-en-Provence Festival, later revived at the Met with Nina Stemme and Lise Davidsen. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Opens with four notes 'D, down to A, up to long F, D' representing Agamemnon; ends with Elektra dancing herself to death

works.name: Der Rosenkavalier
works.description: Strauss's 1911 comic opera (libretto by Hofmannsthal), set in 18th-century Vienna. Centers on the Marschallin (a married noblewoman afraid of aging), her young lover Octavian (a 'breeches role'), the boorish Baron Ochs, and Sophie. In Act II Octavian presents a silver rose to Sophie on behalf of Ochs; they fall in love. The lovers' trio 'Ha mir's gelobt' is sung by three female voices. Act II's duet 'Mit ihren Augen voll Tränen' is interrupted by Baron Ochs. Ends with Octavian and Sophie singing 'Ist ein Traum' after the Marschallin releases Octavian. Wikipedia

clues.clue: The Marschallin stops clocks at night out of fear of aging; the opera opens with wide horn arpeggios and sighing strings representing lovemaking
examples: The opening of an opera by this composer uses wide horn arpeggios and sighing string figures to represent lovemaking between two characters, one of whom stops clocks in the night because of her fear of aging

clues.clue: The lecherous Baron Ochs interrupts a love duet in Act II; Octavian (Mariandl/breeches role) is allowed by the Marschallin to pursue Sophie
examples: Two lovers sing the duet 'Mit ihren augen voll Tranen' in act two of an opera by this composer before being interrupted by the lecherous Baron Ochs
The title breeches-role character of another opera by this composer is allowed by the Marschallin to pursue the hand of Sophie

clues.clue: Three female voices sing the trio 'Ha mir's gelobt' (Octavian, Sophie, Marschallin); Octavian and Sophie sing 'Ist ein Traum' as the Marschallin bids farewell
examples: A love affair first introduced in Act II with an oboe and horn duet in one of this composer's operas is later celebrated by a trio of female voices that sing 'Ha mirs gelob't'
Octavian and Sophie sing the love duet 'Ist ein traum' after the Marschallin bids Octavian farewell

works.name: Ariadne auf Naxos
works.description: Strauss's 1912/1916 opera (libretto by Hofmannsthal). Its prologue (frame story) presents a wealthy host who orders that both a serious opera seria and a comedy troupe must perform simultaneously — an argument about whether music or words matter more (Flamand vs. Olivier). The coloratura soprano Zerbinetta sings the 12-minute aria 'Grossmächtige Prinzessin' advising Ariadne to get over heartbreak. Strauss and Hofmannsthal co-authored a Le Bourgeois Gentilhomme incidental music suite (with a recomposed Lully minuet) that originally preceded the opera. Wikipedia

clues.clue: An opera seria and a commedia dell'arte play must be performed simultaneously in its frame story (prologue); the host demands both be compressed to allow time for fireworks
examples: An opera seria and a commedia dell'arte play must be performed simultaneously in the frame story to his opera Ariadne auf Naxos
In the prologue to this opera, two pieces of entertainment are ordered to be performed simultaneously to leave time for fireworks

clues.clue: Flamand (musician) argues against Olivier (poet) that music is more powerful than words in an opening rehearsal scene

clues.clue: Zerbinetta sings the 12-minute coloratura aria 'Grossmächtige Prinzessin,' advising Ariadne about getting over heartbreak, ending with a rondo beginning 'Als ein Gott'
examples: This composer wrote a rondo beginning with the words 'Als ein Gott' to conclude a 12-minute-long coloratura aria in which Zerbinetta advises another woman about getting over heartbreak; that aria is 'Grossmachtige Prinzessin'

works.name: Symphonia Domestica
works.description: Strauss's 1904 four-movement tone poem depicting domestic life, with three themes representing a father, mother, and child. Wind trills represent a clock striking 7 a.m. in the triple fugue. Criticized savagely by César Cui. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Richard Strauss (1864–1949) was a German Late Romantic composer best known for his orchestral tone poems and operas. He began his conducting career at the Meiningen Court Orchestra as Hans von Bülow's assistant. With librettist Hugo von Hofmannsthal (whom he called a collaborator of genius despite frequent friction) he produced six major operas: Elektra, Der Rosenkavalier, Ariadne auf Naxos, Die Frau ohne Schatten, Die ägyptische Helena, and Arabella. He also co-founded the Salzburg Festival with Hofmannsthal and director Max Reinhardt. His wife was soprano Pauline de Ahna.

His tone poems — a genre he essentially defined — include Also Sprach Zarathustra (1896, based on Nietzsche; famous for the C-G-C sunrise fanfare used in 2001: A Space Odyssey and Elvis Presley concerts; ends with an unresolved clash between B major and C major), Till Eulenspiegel's Merry Pranks (1895; features Till's F major 6/8 solo horn theme and a death-scene with descending trombone seventh and D clarinet squeal), Don Juan, Death and Transfiguration (1889; heartbeat-triplet opening; 'transfiguration motif' F–Bb–C–D–high D–C later quoted in Four Last Songs), Don Quixote (1897; flutter-tongued horns for bleating sheep; the piece Bernstein learned from a sickbed before his 1943 Carnegie Hall debut), Ein Heldenleben (1898; autobiographical, E-flat major; tubas in parallel fifths for critics; 'Works of Peace' quoting earlier Strauss works), An Alpine Symphony (1915; 22 sections; wind machine, thundersheet, 12 offstage horns, heckelphone, cowbells; Karajan's recording was the first CD pressed), and Symphonia Domestica (1904; three themes for father, mother, child; wind-trill clock at 7 a.m.).

Late works include Metamorphosen (1945; 23 solo strings; quotes Eroica funeral march with 'In Memoriam' written in the score; inspired by the bombing of the Vienna Opera House) and Four Last Songs (1948; soprano and orchestra; primarily Hesse texts; quotes 'transfiguration motif' at 'Is this, perhaps, death?'; Jessye Norman's recording with Masur and the Leipzig Gewandhaus is landmark).

His operas: Salome (1905; his own libretto after Wilde; Dance of the Seven Veils; polytonal 'sickening chord' as Salome kisses Jochanaan's head; opens with ambiguous clarinet scale), Elektra (1909; Hofmannsthal libretto; opens D–A–F–D for Agamemnon; 'Elektra chord' polytonal E/C-sharp; Elektra dances herself to death; Chéreau's 2013 Aix-en-Provence production his last work), Der Rosenkavalier (1911; Marschallin fears aging, stops clocks; Octavian breeches role; Baron Ochs; trio 'Ha mir's gelobt'; Octavian and Sophie sing 'Ist ein Traum'), and Ariadne auf Naxos (opera seria + commedia dell'arte performed simultaneously; Zerbinetta's 12-minute coloratura aria 'Grossmächtige Prinzessin').

During the Nazi era Strauss served as Reichsmusikkammer president partly to protect his Jewish daughter-in-law Alice, but was dismissed when his letter to Zweig asking 'if he or Mozart consciously wrote Aryan music' was intercepted. He still wrote the Olympic Hymn for the 1936 Berlin Games. He was sued by Luigi Denza for using 'Funiculi, Funicula' in Aus Italien under the mistaken belief it was a folk song. He once described himself as 'the last mountain of a large mountain range.' George Szell said he conducted as if waiting for the card game that followed the performance.

cards.front: Composer: co-founded the Salzburg Festival with Hugo von Hofmannsthal and Max Reinhardt
Composer: served as president of the Reichsmusikkammer partly to protect his Jewish daughter-in-law Alice; dismissed after an intercepted letter asking if he or Mozart 'consciously wrote Aryan music'
Composer: sued by Luigi Denza for using 'Funiculi, Funicula' in Aus Italien, mistakenly thinking it was a folk song
Composer: collaborated with Stefan Zweig on Die schweigsame Frau, based on a Ben Jonson play, which the Nazis opposed because Zweig was Jewish
Composer: wrote his oboe concerto after an encounter with American soldier John de Lancie; also composed a duet-concertino for clarinet, bassoon, and strings as one of his last works
Tone Poem: opens with ascending 'C-G-C' brass fanfare depicting sunrise; contains movements 'Of Science' (fully chromatic fugue) and 'Song of the Night Wanderer'; used to open 2001: A Space Odyssey
Tone Poem: ends with an unresolved clash between B major (high winds/strings) and C major (low strings), representing humanity vs. the universe

Tone Poem: six sections including 'The Hero's Adversaries' (critics represented by tubas/winds in parallel fifths) and 'Works of Peace' (quoting earlier Strauss compositions); composed in E-flat major
Tone Poem: 22-section tone poem; requires wind machine, thundersheet, 12 offstage horns, heckelphone, cowbells, and organ; Herbert von Karajan's recording was the first CD ever pressed
cards.work: Also Sprach Zarathustra / An Alpine Symphony – General Tone Poems

cards.front: Tone Poem: first section opens with a descending B-flat minor scale where each note is held by the strings until the entire scale is heard simultaneously
cards.work: Also Sprach Zarathustra / An Alpine Symphony – General Tone Poems

cards.front: Tone Poem: Till's theme in F major 6/8 for solo horn; death scene features a descending seventh in trombones/tuba followed by D clarinet squeal and flute trill
Tone Poem: opens with a 'heartbeat' theme (triplets on E-flat/G dyad with gaps); 'transfiguration motif' F–B-flat–C–D–high D–C; Strauss said on his deathbed that it had accurately depicted his death

Tone Poem: fantastic variations on a knightly theme; bleating sheep depicted by flutter-tongued horns; Leonard Bernstein learned passages for his 1943 Carnegie Hall debut from a sickbed coaching by Bruno Walter
Tone Poem: written for 23 solo strings; quotes the Eroica funeral march with 'In Memoriam' written in the score; composed after the bombing of the Vienna Opera House
Song Cycle: soprano cycle primarily to Hesse texts; quotes the 'transfiguration motif' after the soprano sings 'Is this, perhaps, death?'; despite its name, not the last piece Strauss wrote
Song Cycle: Jessye Norman's recording with Kurt Masur and the Leipzig Gewandhaus Orchestra is among the most celebrated

Opera: the title character performs the 'Dance of the Seven Veils' then demands John the Baptist's head on a silver platter; a polytonal chord sounds as she kisses the severed head; she is then crushed to death
Opera: opens with a clarinet scale ambiguous between C-sharp major and G major; the 'sickening chord' combines F-sharp major over A dominant 7th

Opera: opens with four notes 'D, down to A, up to long F, D' representing the title character's murdered father; uses a polytonal 'Elektra chord' implying both E major and C-sharp major; ends with the title character dancing herself to death

Opera: the Marschallin stops clocks at night out of fear of aging; the lecherous Baron Ochs interrupts the Act II love duet 'Mit ihren Augen voll Tränen'; three female voices sing the trio 'Ha mir's gelobt'
Opera: opera seria and commedia dell'arte troupe must perform simultaneously in the frame story; Zerbinetta sings the 12-minute coloratura aria 'Grossmächtige Prinzessin'
Tone Poem: four-movement tone poem depicting domestic life; three themes represent father, mother, and child; wind trills represent a clock striking 7 a.m. before the triple fugue

recursive_suggestions: Salome (Auditory Fine Arts) — rich enough for its own entry with extended clue sets about the Dance of the Seven Veils and the sickening chord
Der Rosenkavalier (Auditory Fine Arts) — major Strauss opera with its own question set
Also Sprach Zarathustra (Auditory Fine Arts) — Strauss's most famous tone poem, appears as independent answerline
Hugo von Hofmannsthal (Literature or Fine Arts) — Strauss's principal librettist for six operas; clued in many Richard Strauss questions
